nativeRange.habitat: Lowland tropical rainforest streams in Sri Lanka's southwestern wet zone (Kelani and Nilwala river basins). Characterized by clear to slightly-stained, shallow, slow-flowing water with low conductivity, leaf litter substrate, and heavy overhanging vegetation; found at elevations up to approximately 300 m.

diet.notes: Tends to accept a broad range of foods. A diet of quality dried flakes or granules with plant or algal content forms a good base, supplemented regularly with small live or frozen foods such as bloodworm, Daphnia, Artemia, and micro worms. Offering fresh vegetables (peas, blanched spinach, zucchini) has been documented as beneficial. Wild populations are thought to forage on algae, detritus, small invertebrates, and organic matter in stream substrates.

avoidWith: nippy species that may harass them
very small shrimp (may be picked at)

imageCandidates.notes: Derek Ramsey (Ram-Man), 800x457. Sharp, iconic in-focus male in full nuptial colour showing the diagnostic ruby-red head/anterior and dark barred body; whole fish framed. Lower resolution but the crispest, most colour-accurate specimen.
NasserHalaweh, 1572x1179. In-focus planted-tank male with red head and iridescent scaling, additional individuals visible behind (schooling context). Slightly softer than the recommended pick.

Alistair Spearing, 2592x1944. Large shoal showing many individuals with ruby-red heads and vertical black bars, representing the species' schooling display; hazy water so kept as a non-recommended group shot.

summary: A Sri Lankan endemic barb where males develop a vivid ruby-red head and intensely darkened body during courtship displays. Generally considered peaceful and well suited to a community aquarium, though the species is best kept by hobbyists with some prior freshwater experience.
careNotes: Reclassified from Puntius to Pethia in 2012, reflecting a broader revision of South Asian barbs within Cyprinidae. The species is endemic to Sri Lanka and listed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List due to restricted range, habitat loss from deforestation, and historical collection pressure; the majority of aquarium stock available today is commercially farm-raised. Males are among the more visually striking freshwater barbs — at rest they show a dark body with three or four vertical black bars on a silvery ground, but during spawning displays or competitive interaction the head and anterior body flush a deep ruby red while the body darkens dramatically. Color intensity tends to increase with larger group sizes; keeping 8–10 or more is generally advised for the best visual results. Females remain comparatively drab (yellowish body with black bars) and are typically plumper when gravid. The species tends to inhabit the mid-water column and appreciates a tank with dim lighting, dark substrate, floating plants, leaf litter, and driftwood — conditions that reflect the shaded lowland rainforest streams of its native range. Though generally peaceful, males have been known to engage in chasing and display behavior among themselves; providing plenty of cover and a balanced sex ratio helps distribute attention. Other closely related South Asian barbs popular in the aquarium trade include the Cherry Barb (Puntius titteya), Rosy Barb (Pethia conchonius), and Odessa Barb (Pethia padamya).
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers with no parental care. Adults will consume eggs and fry if not removed promptly. A dedicated spawning tank of around 40–60 L with soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, low hardness), dim lighting, and a substrate of fine-leaved plants (Java moss) or a spawning mop over a mesh bottom tends to work well. A conditioned pair or trio (one male, two females) is typically introduced in the evening; spawning generally occurs in the morning. Females have been reported to scatter upwards of 100 eggs per session, which typically hatch within 24–48 hours at 24–26°C. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or commercial fry foods initially before graduating to micro worms and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Breeding is generally rated moderate difficulty — triggering spawning is straightforward, but fry-rearing demands attention.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source (Aquarium Co-Op has no dedicated Black Ruby Barb care page; SeriouslyFish used as primary per fallback rule). Gave minimum tank base 80×30 cm (72 L), temperature 20–27°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 36–268 ppm (~2–15 dGH), adult size 50–55 mm, Cyprinidae/Cypriniformes taxonomy, Sri Lanka endemic (Kelani + Nilwala basins). Breeding: egg-scatterers, 24–48 h hatch.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (6.0 cm TL male), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–6.5, hardness 5–12 dH, taxonomy (Cyprinidae/Cypriniformes), native range (Kelani to Nilwala basins, Sri Lanka), IUCN Vulnerable (assessed 2019). FishBase max size of 6.0 cm used as upper bound; temperature and pH ranges averaged across sources.

additional.name: Aquadiction
additional.notes: Cross-verified water parameters: temperature 22–28°C, pH 5.5–7.5, gH 5–12 dGH, TDS 36–268. Lifespan up to 6 years. Confirmed min group size 6, schooling behavior, peaceful temperament, mid swim zone. One FAQ entry citing 8 cm maximum size was treated as an outlier inconsistent with all other sources.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Confirmed size 2–2.5 in (5.1–6.4 cm), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–6.5, tank minimum 30 gal (114 L), lifespan ~5 years, omnivore diet with plant-matter emphasis. Care level listed as moderate; averaged with beginner (Aquadiction) → intermediate.

fish.breedingNotes: The Black Ruby Barb is an egg-scatterer that spawns readily in a conditioned planted tank with soft, slightly acidic water, fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop, and dim lighting. Adults will consume their own eggs if left in the tank, so parents should be removed promptly after spawning. Fry are very small when they first hatch and require infusoria or a commercial fry food initially, graduating to micro worms and Artemia nauplii as they grow.
